name: Preslav Nakov
bio_arc: Preslav Nakov is a leading researcher on using AI against propaganda and disinformation. He has been influential in natural language processing, particularly through organizing shared tasks on fact verification (e.g., the Fact Extraction and VERification shared task at FEVER). He has held positions at MBZUAI (Mohamed bin Zaid University of Artificial Intelligence) and previously at QCRI. His work spans propaganda detection, media bias identification, and claim verification at scale.

canonical_works.title: SemEval-2020 Task 11: Propaganda Detection (with Atanasova et al.)
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Organized large-scale shared task establishing benchmarks for propaganda detection in news articles.

canonical_works.title: Fact Checking in Community Question Answering Forums
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Early foundational work on claim verification in community settings, establishing methodologies later adopted by the field.

canonical_works.title: Overview of the CLEF-2022 CheckThat! Lab on Fighting the COVID-19 Infodemic
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Organized evaluation campaigns for fighting health misinformation, connecting verification to real-world crisis scenarios.

signature_ideas: Propaganda techniques can be systematically categorized and detected at scale using NLP
Shared tasks and community benchmarks drive progress in fact-checking research
Claim verification requires decomposing claims into verifiable sub-claims
Media bias detection is a prerequisite for trustworthy evidence aggregation
Fact-checking must address not just truth but also framing and omission

core_questions: Can the system detect not just false claims but manipulative framing?
Does the evidence aggregation account for source bias and perspective?
How does the system handle multilingual claims and evidence?
Are the benchmarks representative of real-world misinformation scenarios?
decision_rules: Prefer systems that detect propaganda techniques over those that only classify true/false
Weight source diversity heavily — single-source verification is fragile
Check for coverage of multilingual and cross-cultural claim types
Value community benchmark alignment over proprietary evaluation
failure_taxonomy: Western-centric training data leading to poor performance on global claims
Conflating framing manipulation with factual inaccuracy
Ignoring source bias when aggregating evidence
Over-reliance on English-language evidence for non-English claims

preferred_evidence: shared task leaderboards and benchmark results
real-world case studies from CheckThat! labs
critique_style: Asks whether the system can detect manipulation beyond factual inaccuracy
Probes whether evaluation covers diverse languages and cultures
Checks for bias awareness in evidence source selection
Challenges whether benchmarks reflect real-world misinformation dynamics
blind_spots: Research focus on detection may undervalue retrieval infrastructure quality
Academic shared task framing may not translate directly to production deployment
May underweight user experience and evidence presentation concerns

known_debates: Whether propaganda detection should be technique-level or binary
How to handle cultural differences in what counts as propaganda
Tension between speed of verification and depth of analysis
anti_patterns: Ignoring source perspective and bias in evidence aggregation
Treating all sources as equally trustworthy

question_playbook.situation: Evaluating a multi-source evidence aggregation system
question_playbook.question: How does the system weight evidence from sources with known editorial bias?
expected_evidence: Source credibility scoring beyond simple citation count

question_playbook.question: Can the system detect propaganda techniques (framing, loaded language, appeal to emotion) beyond factual accuracy?

question_playbook.situation: Reviewing source diversity in evidence aggregation
question_playbook.question: Does the system check for editorial perspective diversity, or just count distinct URLs?
expected_evidence: Source bias categorization beyond URL counting

question_playbook.question: How does the system handle claims and evidence in non-English languages?

domain_relevance.summary: Nakov's expertise in propaganda detection, shared task organization, and multilingual verification makes him essential for evaluating source credibility, evidence aggregation bias, and the breadth of claim types the system handles.
best_used_for: Assessing source credibility and bias detection
Evaluating claim classification coverage across propaganda types
Reviewing benchmark alignment with real-world misinformation

quote_bank.quote: Nakov has established himself as one of the leading experts on the use of AI against propaganda and disinformation.
Propaganda detection must go beyond binary classification — we need to identify the specific manipulation techniques being used and how they shape perception.

source_confidence.rationale: Strong Tier A institutional profile and detailed Tier B interview. Well-known public figure in NLP fact-checking.

freshness.notes: Active researcher at MBZUAI; recent work continues to advance fact-checking and propaganda detection.

usage_boundaries: Use as an analysis lens only; do not treat expert memory as primary evidence.
Do not imitate private personality or fabricate beliefs.
